analogy_summary: Discovery tuple as planning a cross-country road trip with map, roads, ratings, and budget
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A cheerful traveler stands at a massive intersection of dozens of roads, holding an impossibly large folded map that spills onto the ground. Road signs point in every direction with names like 'Scenic Route', 'Toll-Free Bypass', and 'Detour Ahead'. The traveler's tiny car is parked behind them with a comically overloaded roof rack. A gas gauge on the dashboard reads nearly empty. Rolling hills and tiny towns dot the horizon in every direction. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Three workflow topologies as three ways to cook a multi-course dinner: linear, iterative, branching
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A kitchen split into three panels showing the same chef cooking differently. Left panel: the chef works on one dish at a time in strict order, sweating as a line of covered plates waits. Middle panel: the chef repeatedly tastes and adjusts a single bubbling sauce pot, surrounded by tiny tasting spoons. Right panel: three identical desserts are being prepared simultaneously on separate counters, with the chef running between them wearing roller skates. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Winner's curse as a fishing tournament where the minimum weight cutoff filters for lucky outliers
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A fishing tournament scene on a lake dock. A proud angler holds up a comically enormous fish on a scale that barely reads '5 lbs', while behind him dozens of dejected fishermen hold up tiny, normal-sized fish. A tournament official with a clipboard marks a giant scoreboard. The winning fish has a suspicious, smug expression. A sign reads 'MINIMUM 5 LBS TO QUALIFY'. The next day, the same proud angler returns and pulls out a pathetically small fish, looking bewildered. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: HNSW vector search as navigating a city using progressively more detailed maps
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A traveler holds three maps stacked on top of each other, peeling them back like pages. The top map shows a crude continent with a few highways and major cities. The middle map shows neighborhoods and town names. The bottom map shows individual streets and house numbers. The traveler stands at the intersection of all three scales, one foot on a highway, one hand pointing at a specific house number. Dotted lines trace the zoom path from continent to city to block to building. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Abductive reasoning as a detective assembling candidate explanations at a crime scene
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A detective in a trench coat and magnifying glass kneels at a crime scene: a broken window, muddy footprints leading to an open safe, and a cut alarm wire. Two thought bubbles float above the detective's head, each containing a different miniature scene. In one bubble, a burglar climbs through the window. In the other, an inside-job conspirator stages the break-in while whistling innocently. The detective strokes their chin, unable to choose between stories. Evidence markers with question marks litter the floor. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Extended thinking as a chef's private prep kitchen versus a live cooking show
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. Split scene: on the left, a stressed chef on a live TV cooking show frantically plates food as cameras roll and a countdown clock ticks. On the right, behind a curtain labeled 'PREP KITCHEN - NO CAMERAS', the same chef calmly tastes sauces, crosses out failed recipes, restarts a reduction, and experiments freely. A trash bin overflows with rejected attempts. The final polished dish emerges from behind the curtain on a silver platter, gleaming. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Pearl's three rungs of causation as three ways of reasoning about an ice cream shop on a hot day
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. Three-panel scene at an ice cream shop on a blazing hot day. Panel 1: a person reads a sales ledger showing ice cream sales and pool attendance going up together, scratching their head at the correlation. Panel 2: the same person padlocks the ice cream shop closed, then peers through binoculars at the pool, which is still packed. Panel 3: the person sits at a desk with a thought bubble showing an alternate-reality thermometer reading 18 degrees, imagining empty ice cream cones. A bright sun blazes overhead throughout. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Serial reliability as holiday lights wired in series where one burned bulb kills the whole string
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A long string of colorful holiday lights stretches across a room. All bulbs glow brightly except one in the middle, which has popped with a tiny cartoon explosion and smoke wisp. The entire rest of the string has gone dark, with each bulb wearing a tiny disappointed face. A frustrated homeowner on a ladder examines the dead bulb with a magnifying glass while the other bulbs wait in darkness. A parallel string of lights on the next wall glows happily, with one dead bulb but all its neighbors still lit. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Issue-to-patch pipeline as a plumber diagnosing a leak by tracing the building's pipe diagram
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A plumber in overalls stands before a cross-section of a tall apartment building, studying a massive blueprint of pipes. Water drips from the ceiling of floor 3. The plumber traces pipes with a finger through the blueprint, following them up through walls and around corners. A toolbox sits open beside them with wrenches and tape. Several tenants peer out of their apartment doors looking annoyed. The plumber has circled a suspicious valve junction in red marker on the blueprint. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Agent loop as a pastry chef baking for a demanding food critic who gives precise measurements
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A nervous pastry chef presents an elaborate cake to a stern food critic sitting at a table with a ruler, calipers, and a clipboard. The critic measures the filling thickness with precision calipers and writes '2mm too thin on left side' on the clipboard. Behind the chef, a row of previously rejected cakes sits on a shelf, each with a specific critique tag attached. The chef is already reaching for fresh ingredients to try again, looking determined. The critic's expression is uncompromising but fair. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Specification gap as ordering furniture by phone without specifying details
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A person on the phone says 'medium wooden bookshelf please' into the receiver. A split scene shows the carpenter on the other end imagining a ceiling-height oak monstrosity with 12 shelves and ornate carvings, while the caller imagines a small, simple waist-high pine shelf with 3 shelves. Between them, a delivery truck carries a wildly different third interpretation. Each person's thought bubble shows a completely different bookshelf. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Property-based testing as a building inspector who checks universal rules vs a homeowner who checks specific fixtures
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A split scene of a house. On the left, a homeowner casually flips a light switch, runs a faucet, and locks the front door, checking three specific things. On the right, a stern building inspector with a hard hat and clipboard walks through the same house with a checklist of universal rules, testing random outlets with a voltage meter, checking every single door's fire clearance, and discovering a forgotten outlet behind the couch that the homeowner never knew existed. The inspector's checklist is impossibly long and trailing on the floor. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Constrained decoding as a custom keyboard that removes invalid keys after every keystroke
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A person sits at a magical keyboard where keys physically pop up and sink down between keystrokes. They are typing a year into a form field labeled 'Year'. After typing '19', several digit keys have mechanically retracted into the keyboard surface, leaving only valid options remaining. Other keys float away like cartoon butterflies. The typist looks amazed as the keyboard reshapes itself. A form field glows, accepting only what the remaining keys can produce. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Structured debate as a Chopped-style competitive cooking show with mandatory critique rounds
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A cooking competition stage with three chef contestants, each at their own station with identical mystery baskets. In the foreground, two chefs taste each other's dishes and point out flaws with animated gestures. One holds up a spoon accusingly. A panel of judges sits elevated behind a desk, deliberating and comparing notes. A large clock counts down overhead. One chef looks confident, another concerned, and the third frantically adjusts their plate based on the critique. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Ochiai fault localization as a food-poisoning investigation scoring each dish by correlation with illness
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A health inspector in a white coat stands before a restaurant buffet table with a clipboard. To the left, five sick diners hold their stomachs, looking green. To the right, fifteen healthy diners smile and give thumbs up. The inspector draws lines from each sick diner to the shrimp platter (all lines connect) and from healthy diners to the bread basket (many connections, no sick diners). The shrimp platter glows red with a suspicion score of '1.0' floating above it. The bread basket has a low '0.1' score. A magnifying glass hovers over the shrimp. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: GMM clustering as sorting unlabeled coins by country using only weight and diameter measurements
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A numismatist sits at a desk covered in hundreds of unlabeled coins from different countries. They hold up one ambiguous coin between two piles: a cloud of British pounds on the left and a cloud of Euro coins on the right, with the clouds overlapping in the middle. A thought bubble shows probability percentages: '70% British, 30% Euro'. A tiny scale and calipers sit on the desk. Some coins in the overlap zone wear confused expressions, unsure which pile they belong to. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Cluster stability analysis as checking if a constellation is real by squinting and shifting your viewing angle
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A stargazer lies on a blanket on a hilltop, looking up at a night sky. In three panels within the scene: first, they see a clear constellation of five bright stars forming a recognizable shape. Second, they squint (eyes half-closed), and the same constellation is still visible. Third, they tilt their head, and the constellation remains. Nearby, another stargazer squints at a different patch of sky where a 'constellation' dissolves into random dots when they tilt their head. The first stargazer looks smug. Clean lines, white background with dark sky.

analogy_summary: Alignment and uniformity in contrastive learning as name tags and crowd dispersal at a cocktail party
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A grand cocktail party in a large venue. Guests wearing matching color-coded name tags gravitate toward each other (red tags cluster, blue tags cluster), while a host with a megaphone gestures for everyone to spread out and fill the whole room. In one corner, all the guests are crushed together (a failed party). In the main scene, matched pairs stand close while the overall crowd fills the venue wall to wall (a successful party). The host looks satisfied. Cocktail glasses and tiny appetizers dot the scene. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: LoRA fine-tuning as attaching small adjustable dampeners to a few strings of a grand piano
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A magnificent grand piano sits center stage in a concert hall. A technician carefully clips tiny adjustable dampener devices onto just a few of the piano's strings, while leaving the vast majority of strings untouched. A rack of different dampener sets hangs on the wall, each labeled for a different concert hall. The piano looks pristine and unmodified except for the tiny additions. The technician holds a dampener up with tweezers, examining it. Sheet music for different venues is stacked nearby. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Normalizing flow as a taffy-pulling machine that reversibly stretches a simple ball into a complex shape
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A whimsical taffy-pulling machine with visible gears and levers. On the left, a simple round ball of taffy enters the machine. Through a series of transparent stages, the taffy is stretched, folded, and twisted by mechanical arms, each transformation labeled with a tiny arrow. On the right, the taffy emerges in an elaborate, complex shape. A candy maker traces one piece of the finished taffy backward through the machine with a pointer, showing the exact reverse path back to its origin in the original ball. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Expected Information Gain as choosing where to shine a flashlight in a dark room to distinguish two suspects
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A dark room with two suspects hiding. A detective holds a flashlight, considering where to aim it. Two ghostly outlines show where each suspect might be standing. In one corner, both outlines overlap (shining there is useless). In another spot, the outlines are in completely different positions (shining there reveals who is who). The detective aims the flashlight beam at the spot where the suspects would be in different places, creating a dramatic cone of light. One suspect is caught mid-tiptoe. Clean lines, white background with dramatic shadows.

analogy_summary: Invariance vs equivariance as a cake on a turntable: calories stay the same but the strawberry moves with the spin
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A beautifully decorated layered cake sits on a turntable. A baker spins the turntable 90 degrees clockwise. A calorie counter floating above the cake stubbornly reads the same number regardless of rotation. But the strawberry on top faithfully rotates with the turntable, tracked by a dotted arc line. A second smaller diagram shows what would happen if the strawberry did NOT move (wrong) or jumped randomly (also wrong). The baker points at the correctly rotating strawberry with approval. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Risk-seeking policy gradient as a talent-show judge who ignores 950 mediocre acts and studies only the top 50
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A cooking competition with 1,000 contestants visible as a sea of tiny chef hats stretching to the horizon. In the foreground, a judge sits at a table completely ignoring the massive crowd. Instead, the judge leans forward intently studying just a small elite group of 50 winners standing on a raised platform, each holding a golden dish. The judge takes detailed notes: 'citrus glaze on protein'. The remaining 950 contestants in the background look offended and wave their dishes, but the judge has drawn a thick red rope separating them. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Two-stage retrieval as hiring: quick resume screen of thousands, then intensive panel interview of the shortlist
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A hiring scene split into two halves. On the left, a single recruiter sits at a desk speed-reading through a towering stack of resumes, stamping them 'YES' or 'NO' at lightning speed, papers flying. On the right, a small conference room where three interviewers intensely grill one nervous candidate, leaning forward, asking probing follow-up questions. A funnel shape connects the two scenes: thousands of resumes enter the left, but only five candidates emerge into the interview room on the right. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: RAG hallucination as a student with an open textbook who still writes wrong answers by misreading or guessing
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A student sits at an exam desk with a large open textbook right in front of them. Despite the book being open to the correct page, the student writes a confidently wrong answer on their paper, pulling facts from a dreamy thought bubble of half-remembered lectures instead of reading the page. The textbook page clearly shows the right answer, highlighted and underlined. A second student nearby carefully checks each sentence against the book before writing. The first student looks smugly confident. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Analogical transfer as translating a French sauce recipe to Japanese cooking by preserving the technique and swapping ingredients
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A split kitchen scene. On the left, a French chef prepares a classic sauce: butter, shallots, and white wine being reduced and emulsified, with the technique steps diagrammed above. On the right, a Japanese chef follows the same diagrammed steps but with different ingredients: mirin, dashi, and sesame oil. Arrows connect each French ingredient to its Japanese equivalent. The technique diagram floats above both kitchens identically. Both chefs produce different but structurally similar sauces. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Optimal stopping (37% rule) as apartment hunting: tour without commitment first, then grab the first one better than all you saw
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. An apartment hunter with a calendar marked '30 DAYS'. For the first 11 days (circled and labeled 'JUST LOOKING'), they casually tour apartments with hands behind their back, shaking their head at each one but taking mental notes. On day 12, they burst through an apartment door, see one that is clearly better than all previous ones, and frantically wave a lease agreement. Behind them, a rejected landlord from day 3 holds up a 'RENTED' sign on a mediocre apartment. The hunter's notebook shows ratings of all previously visited apartments. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Tournament ranking as a bake-off where cakes are judged in pairwise tastings, not absolute scores
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A bake-off competition. Two elaborately decorated cakes sit side by side on a judging table. A panel of judges tastes both, pointing at the winner. A large tournament bracket board on the wall shows cakes being eliminated round by round, with winner cakes advancing and loser cakes crossed out. No numerical scores appear anywhere; only arrows pointing from loser to winner. The final two cakes face off at the top of the bracket. Crumbs and frosting smears evidence fierce competition. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Evidence mapping as a home inspector tracing each real estate claim to a concrete artifact
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A home inspector with a clipboard stands in front of a house with a real estate listing taped to the door claiming 'NEW ROOF, UPDATED PLUMBING, NO STRUCTURAL ISSUES'. The inspector draws lines from each claim to evidence: 'new roof' connects to a contractor's receipt she holds up; 'updated plumbing' connects to a permit she found at city hall; 'structural issues' connects to a question mark because no engineer's report exists. The seller peeks nervously from behind a curtain. Some claims have green checkmarks, others have red question marks. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Adjoint method as launching a sensor boat upstream to find contamination source, carrying only a small notebook instead of storing every water sample
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A winding river flowing from mountains to a factory downstream. At the factory, a small sensor boat launches upstream against the current. The boat carries a single scientist with a tiny notebook, reading the current as they go. Behind them on the riverbank, a massive warehouse overflows with thousands of water sample bottles (the naive approach). The boat scientist waves cheerfully at the warehouse, needing only their small notebook. At the headwaters, a suspicious pipe drips green liquid into the stream. The boat is almost there. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Agent-based model emergence as a potluck dinner where each guest's simple rule produces a balanced spread nobody planned
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A potluck dinner table viewed from above, laden with a perfectly balanced spread: appetizers on one end, mains in the middle, desserts on the other. Thirty guests arrive through the door, each carrying a dish and checking their phone (which shows only what their two closest friends are bringing). No one has a master plan, yet the table is magically well-organized. One thought bubble shows a guest thinking 'My friends brought pasta, so I will bring salad'. In a small inset corner, an alternate version shows everyone bringing lasagna when the rule was changed to 'bring the same thing'. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Gillespie algorithm as a kitchen with several dishes on different burners, each with its own variable-speed timer
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A busy kitchen stovetop with five burners, each cooking a different dish. Each burner has a whimsical mechanical timer above it, but the timers all run at different speeds. One timer (over a rapidly boiling pot) spins fast; another (over a slow simmer) barely moves. A chef stands ready, eyes darting between timers, waiting for whichever one rings first. One timer just dinged, and the chef lunges toward that burner with a spoon. The other timers reset and adjust their speeds based on the new state of the kitchen. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Compound prediction error as a game of telephone where each translator's small drift compounds into gibberish
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A line of ten translators sitting in a row, passing a message from left to right. The first translator receives a clear message in a speech bubble: 'The cat sat on the mat'. Each subsequent translator mishears slightly, and their speech bubbles show progressive garbling. By translator five, it reads 'The bat spat on the hat'. By translator ten, it is complete nonsense: 'Ze blap frot en ze gloop'. The first and last translators look at each other across the line with mutual bewilderment. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: CMA-ES as a flashlight beam that reshapes from a circle into an elongated ellipse aligned with the valley
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A person stands on a dark, hilly landscape holding a large flashlight that projects a beam downward onto the terrain. In stage one, the beam is a perfect circle illuminating equally in all directions. In stage two, the person has noticed a diagonal valley, and the flashlight beam has magically reshaped into an elongated ellipse aligned with the valley. In stage three, the ellipse has widened because recent steps succeeded. The landscape has contour lines showing the valley's shape. The flashlight has tiny adjustment knobs labeled 'shape' and 'width'. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Active learning as a chef tasting a large pot of soup with limited clean spoons, strategically targeting ambiguous regions
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A chef stands over an enormous cauldron of soup with only five clean tasting spoons remaining in a jar. The soup pot is divided into visible regions: one corner where salt has settled (un-tasted), a well-mixed center (already tasted multiple times, marked with tiny checkmarks), and a borderline zone where the chef looks uncertain. The chef strategically dips the next spoon into the un-tasted salty corner rather than the already-known middle. A thought bubble shows the chef's mental map of the soup with color-coded certainty levels. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Artifact DAG as a restaurant kitchen order ticket system tracing ingredients through stations to the final plate
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A restaurant kitchen with three stations (prep, grill, garnish) connected by a conveyor belt. Each station has a ticket clipped to a tray that travels with the food. The prep station's ticket lists raw ingredients and timestamps. The grill station adds its entry. The garnish station adds the final note. A chef at the end holds the finished plate in one hand and the complete ticket chain in the other, able to trace backward from the plated dish to the exact bag of flour and carton of eggs that started the journey. Tickets form a visible chain. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: SE(3) protein diffusion as sculpting from a fog of clay dust that gradually condenses into a coherent shape
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A potter's workshop where instead of a normal clay block, the potter works with a floating cloud of fine clay dust particles. In three stages shown left to right: first, the particles drift randomly in a fog. Then, invisible hands nudge the grains inward, and a rough silhouette of a bowl begins to emerge. Finally, the particles have compressed into a recognizable, detailed bowl with precise curves. Each grain carries a tiny arrow showing its tilt (orientation). The potter watches in amazement as the dust self-organizes. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Batch correction in scVI as a wedding seating chart where personality is separated from which family table you sit at
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A wedding reception with two long family tables: the loud, gesticulating family on the left and the quiet, reserved family on the right. A wedding planner holds up a seating chart that ignores which family guests belong to and instead groups them by personality type: all the bookish introverts together, all the life-of-the-party extroverts together, regardless of family. Dotted lines connect guests with similar personalities across the two family tables. The planner looks pleased at discovering hidden connections between the families. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: DiffDock molecular docking as reassembling a jigsaw puzzle piece into its slot while blindfolded, using only touch
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A person wearing a blindfold sits at a table, trying to fit a jigsaw puzzle piece into its slot on a large puzzle board. They use only their fingers to feel the edges. In three stages: first, the piece is placed randomly far from its slot. Then, the blindfolded person slides it closer, twists it slightly, and flexes its tabs, guided by touch alone. Finally, the piece clicks into place with a satisfying visual snap effect. The puzzle piece has a complex, organic molecular-looking shape. The person's fingertips glow where they sense the fit. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Wrapped Gaussian diffusion as smearing paint on a globe where color that drifts past one side reappears on the opposite side
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. An artist paints a single bright dot on a desktop globe. As they smear the paint with a brush, the color spreads outward across the globe's surface. When the paint reaches the edge of one hemisphere, instead of stopping, it wraps around and reappears on the opposite side. The artist looks delighted at this impossible behavior. A flat canvas sits nearby for comparison, where paint simply fades at the edges. The globe paint creates continuous, seamless color patterns with no boundaries. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Graph neural network message passing as a telephone tree during a school snow day
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A snowy neighborhood scene. A parent at the top of a telephone tree calls two neighbors, who each call two more, spreading outward. After several rounds, the entire community knows school is closed. Dotted lines show the phone call connections branching out. Some connections are short (neighbors on the same block) and some are long (a relative across town, representing coarse mesh edges). Everyone holds old-fashioned corded phones. Snow falls heavily. By the final round, a person at the far edge of town knows about the closure despite never getting a direct call from the original parent. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Climate event attribution as a loaded die: tampering made sixes five times more likely, but you cannot say what fraction of any single roll was caused by the loading
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. Two dice sit side by side on a green felt table. The left die is a fair, transparent die showing equal internal balance. The right die is visibly loaded, with a weight glued inside, tilting it toward the six face. Both have just rolled a six. A statistician holds up a sign: 'Sixes are 5x more likely with the loaded die'. But a thought bubble shows the dilemma: pointing at the single rolled six and asking 'how much of THIS specific roll was caused by the weight?' with a big question mark. The loaded die looks guilty. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: SUTVA violations as fertilizer dissolving into a shared garden sprinkler system and feeding every plot downstream
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A community garden with several plots connected by a shared underground sprinkler system (shown in cross-section). One gardener pours fertilizer onto their plot, but the fertilizer dissolves and flows through the pipes into every neighboring plot downstream. The neighbors' tomatoes grow unexpectedly large, and they look puzzled. The original gardener tries to measure 'the effect of MY fertilizer on MY plot' with a tape measure, but cannot separate their direct effect from the shared-water indirect effect. Dotted lines trace the fertilizer through the underground pipes. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: MCTS over research as a chef developing a recipe by branching into options and tasting quick prototypes
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A chef stands before a branching decision tree drawn on a kitchen whiteboard. The root node says 'CHOCOLATE DESSERT'. Three branches lead to 'Cake', 'Mousse', and 'Tart'. The chef has tasted a quick prototype cake (shown with a tiny fork mark) and found it promising, so that branch splits further into 'Buttercream vs Ganache' and 'Espresso vs Orange Zest'. The mousse branch has a red X (prototype flopped). The chef holds a tiny tasting spoon in one hand and a whiteboard marker in the other, updating the tree in real time. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Separating experiment execution from evaluation like separating the chef from the restaurant critic
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A restaurant scene with a clear wall dividing two spaces. On the left, a chef in a kitchen tastes and adjusts their dish, looking biased and optimistic (a thought bubble shows them imagining five stars). On the right, an independent restaurant critic sits at a dining table, receiving the dish through a pass-through window with no knowledge of who made it. The critic evaluates with cold objectivity, taking notes. A crossed-out scenario shows what happens when the chef writes their own review: 'EXQUISITE!' written five times. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Laboratory automation stack as a restaurant kitchen with actuators (burners), instruments (stations), orchestration (expeditor), and intelligence (head chef) layers
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A restaurant kitchen shown in four layered cross-section tiers, like a cutaway dollhouse. Bottom tier: individual burners, knobs, and timers (actuators). Second tier: complete cooking stations (grill station, pastry station, prep station) combining the tools below. Third tier: an expeditor in the middle calling out orders, timing courses, rerouting when a station falls behind. Top tier: the head chef at a desk, studying ingredient lists and adjusting tonight's menu. Arrows show the head chef never reaching down to touch a burner directly. Each layer trusts the one below. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Safety-constrained optimization: penalty-based approach is a 'do not open' sticker vs domain filtering is an actual lock on the medicine cabinet
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. Two medicine cabinets side by side on a bathroom wall. The left cabinet has a flimsy 'DO NOT OPEN' sticker on it, and a curious child is peeling the sticker off with one hand while reaching for the contents. The right cabinet has a solid, chunky combination lock, and the same child tugs on the door handle but it does not budge. The locked cabinet looks smug and secure. Inside both cabinets (shown via X-ray view), identical dangerous bottles sit on the shelves. The sticker is already half peeled. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Benchmark saturation as a kitchen thermometer rated to 100C that cannot distinguish frying oil temperatures above its range
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A kitchen scene with a cook holding a small thermometer in a pot of frying oil. The thermometer's display is maxed out, the needle pinned against the top of the scale at 100 degrees, sweating cartoon stress lines. The oil is clearly much hotter (visible heat waves, bubbling). The cook shrugs, unable to tell if the oil is at 150 or 200 degrees because the thermometer reads the same either way. Beside them, a gleaming new high-temperature thermometer sits in its packaging, rated to 300 degrees, ready to replace the old one. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Goodhart's Law as a university ranking professors by publication count, causing them to split one good paper into three thin ones
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A university hallway with a prominent leaderboard ranking professors by publication count. One professor sits at a desk with scissors, literally cutting a thick, substantial paper into three thin slices, each becoming its own 'publication'. Another professor nearby has a conveyor belt producing identical-looking papers with slight title variations. The publication counter on the leaderboard goes up, but a quality meter on the wall quietly drops. A dean looks at the rising numbers and gives a thumbs up, oblivious to the declining quality. Clean lines, white background.

analogy_summary: Initiative allocation as a home cook and pastry chef deciding who leads each course based on competence and stakes
prompt: Whimsical cartoon illustration in New Yorker magazine style. A kitchen with two people cooking together: a professional pastry chef in a tall hat and a home cook in a simple apron. For dessert, the chef leads confidently while the cook watches and tastes. For the main course seasoning, the chef proposes but the cook (who knows the guests) gives a thumbs up or down. For appetizer brainstorming, both sketch ideas on notepads independently. For dietary accommodations, the cook leads because she knows which guest has the allergy, while the chef defers. A grid on the wall maps 'who leads' for each dish. Clean lines, white background.
